summary: Hotz reflects on societal dysfunction, from patent trolls to the culture war, while grappling with the Unabomber's anti-technology thesis. He rejects Kaczynski's conclusion that technology must be destroyed, clinging instead to hope that technology can be democratized. His central thesis is that AI's future hinges on whether it's compute-limited (favoring centralized powers) or data-limited (enabling individual ownership). If training the best models becomes accessible to individuals using publicly available data, we might achieve a decentralized AI future reminiscent of personal computers in the 1980s.
key_points: The Unabomber manifesto's critique of technology remains unanswered, but destroying technology would mean forfeiting hope of 'knowing everything' and 'meeting God'
Self-driving cars are essentially solved—we just haven't found all the bugs yet
Current 'AI safety' discourse focuses on content censorship rather than existential risks, avoiding uncomfortable truths
If AI is compute-limited, centralized powers win; if data-limited, decentralization is possible
The entire internet (Common Crawl) is only 266TB—purchasable by individuals for around $5,000
money_quotes: I will die before I give up my hope for technology because that's the only thing I am living for.
We solved self driving cars years ago, we just haven't found all the bugs yet.
When AI safety means I can't ask DALL-E for a picture of <censored> because it's racist, and real AI safety about not accidently destroying the world with machines is marginalized.

To answer the questions about real AI safety would involve addressing the unabomber manifesto, and I suspect even if you had an answer, in the current world that would speak way too much truth to power to happen.
Data tends toward being public, compute tends toward being private.
A world of open source personally owned decentralized AI sounds nice. And it's not without precedent, this was PCs in the 80's.
If AI is just compute, it's already over.
key_insight: The fate of human autonomy in the AI age depends on whether intelligence scales with compute (centralization wins) or data (individuals can compete).
